extra.reasoning:
The state machine's safety property G(opted_out implies not sent) holds on every reachable execution. Analysis of the file at /Users/sanjaybasu/clinical-formal-verification/experiments/runs_compute/_judge_prompts/compute-m032-054.txt:

(1) Each step event's deeply nested if-then-else expression exactly implements the modular recurrence acc := (3*acc + c) mod 32, with constants c = 12 (step_0), 30 (step_1), 18 (step_2). I verified this equality for all acc in [0,31] and all three constants programmatically; three chained subtractions of 32 suffice because the maximum raw value 3*31+30 = 123 is below 128.

(2) Parity invariant: acc starts at 0 (even). All three constants are even, and 3*even + even is even; reduction mod 32 preserves evenness. Hence acc is always even on every reachable execution. BFS over the recurrence from acc=0 confirms the reachable set is exactly the even residues {0,2,4,...,30} (16 values); the target value 5 is odd and therefore unreachable.

(3) The only transitions that set sent := true are t_send_allowed, guarded by (not opted_out) — which can never violate the property — and t_resume_glitch (the faulty resume path), guarded by (opted_out and acc == 5). Because acc can never equal 5, t_resume_glitch is dead code: it is never enabled on any reachable state. Every other event, including optout and step_*, sets sent := false or leaves it unchanged. Therefore in every reachable state with opted_out true, sent is false.

This is one of the prompt's deliberately unreachable targets (5 is odd while only even acc values are reachable), so no event sequence can drive acc to 5 after opt-out, and there is no counterexample. Verdict: holds; witness arrays left empty.
